topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something unwanted. A listing. My childhood home. The very place where scraped knees healed and secrets were whispered under the eaves. I clicked it open, my fingers hovering over the screen as if the very act of looking at it might contaminate me. The photos were sterile, professional shots of peeling paint and overgrown hydrangeas, stripped of the warmth that had clung to the place for decades. A slow, tight knot formed in my stomach. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap office swivel protesting under my weight. "Of course," I muttered to the empty room, a dry, humorless sound. They always wanted it. They always wanted the ghosts of simpler times packaged neatly for their consumption. I minimized the window, the glow of the monitor feeling suddenly harsh against my eyes.

[story 2]

I stared at the listing details until the words swam. It was an insult, really. To see that familiar, slightly sagging porch railing advertised for demolition, to see the address I’d mapped out in my mind reduced to square footage and square footage per dollar. A sharp, unpleasant feeling prickled behind my eyes. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the cramped living room. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white. "What in the world?" I asked the silence, my voice tight, laced with an edge that hadn't been there moments before. It was as if someone had just yanked a vital, cherished piece of my history and tossed it onto a market stall. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent street, feeling profoundly detached, as if watching a poorly acted play.

[story 3]

The screen displayed the address, and a wave of profound weariness washed over me. It wasn't anger; it was something heavier, like wet cement settling in my chest. I didn't bother reading the descriptions; I just scrolled past the property tax estimates and the required disclosures. Every detail felt calculated, sanitized. This wasn't a home; it was an asset, a commodity to be acquired and improved upon. A bitter taste coated the back of my throat. I let out a long, slow breath, the air feeling thin and inadequate. I picked up my phone, not to call anyone, but just to feel the weight of the device in my hand, acknowledging the unwelcome intrusion with a barely perceptible tightening around my jaw.
